segments.s427-g65: چو at the clause head is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. شیران جنگی is a compound: شیران (figurative 'brave men' or 'lions') modified by جنگی 'warlike'; برآشوفتند is past plural of آشفتن with the prefix بر- giving the sense 'rushed upon' or 'surged at'. The whole segment = 'when the warlike lions/warriors rushed in'.
segments.s427-g66: همی is a classical continuous/emphatic adverb here ('kept' / continuous aspect). بر سر یکدگر کوفتند: بر + سر = 'upon the head(s)' and یکدگر is the reciprocal 'one another'; کوفتند is past plural 'they struck / beat'. Word order places the adverb first, then the prepositional phrase and verb.
segments.s427-g67: در آوردگه gives location 'in/at the arena/battleground' (در + آوردگه). تیز شد is a copular phrase 'became keen/sharp/alert'; مهرنوش is the proper name and functions as the subject following the predicate (poetic word order).
segments.s427-g68: نبودش is نبوده + -ش (clitic) meaning 'she was not' or 'it was not there'. همی here is emphatic 'indeed / surely'. با فرامرز = 'with Faramarz'; توش = تو + ش contracted enclitic meaning 'in it' or 'therein' — together: 'she was not there with Faramarz.' Note the clitic pronouns and archaic contraction.
segments.s427-g69: بزد is the simple past 'he struck' (third person). تیغ follows as the object 'with the sword' (instrumental sense). بر گردن اسپ خویش = 'against/at the neck of his horse' — classical omission of ezâfe and division of خویش into خوی + ش (possessive clitic). The phrase thus marks the target of the blow.
segments.s427-g70: سر (the head) is the direct object of اندرافگند (an archaic/perfect form of اندر افکندن 'to throw/put into, to cast'). بادپای 'swift‑footed' qualifies the person in front of whom the head is cast; پیش = 'before / in front of'. The verb اندرافگند is fused here (archaic poetic form) meaning 'he cast/threw'.
segments.s427-g71: فرامرز is the agent (subject). کردش is کرد + -ش = 'made him' or 'did to him' (causative/active past). پیاده تباه is a nominal phrase 'a ruined footman / ruined on foot' — poetic apposition indicating the victim's state after Faramarz's action.
segments.s427-g72: ز خون = 'from the blood' (ز is the poetic form of از). لعل شد = 'became laleh/ruby' (لعل used metaphorically for deep red), شد = 'became'. خاک آوردگاه = 'the soil/earth of the battlefield' (آوردگاه = battlefield/venue). Word order is topical: cause ('from the blood') first, then result ('became ruby the battlefield's earth').
segments.s427-g57: برفت is a past action meaning 'went/moved forth' (here the intended verb is past 'he went' though the supplied lemma senses are for another root); از میان سپه پیش صف is a prepositional phrase 'from among the host to the front of the line' with the order preposition + noun (از میان سپه) then goal/position (پیش صف). پیش is used spatially 'in front of/before' and صف here means the battle line/formation.
segments.s427-g58: ز (poetic variant of از) marks source or cause: 'from/through.' درد جگر is an idiomatic noun phrase where جگر = 'heart/seat of feeling' and درد = 'pain/anguish' ('the pain of his heart'). بر لب آورده کف is a compound: بر (on/upon) + لب (lip) + آورده (brought, past participle used predicatively) + کف (foam). Grammatically آورده here means 'he had brought' (resultant state: foam on his lips).
segments.s427-g59: وزان سو is a compact poetic locative: وزان = 'from that' and سو = 'side/direction' ('from that side'). فرامرز is the proper name subject. چون + پیل + مست is a simile: چون = 'like/as', پیل = 'elephant', and مست here is figurative 'intoxicated/maddened' (a common epic simile meaning huge and unstoppable). The clause describes Faramarz's manner of coming.
segments.s427-g60: بیامد is the past 'came' (he/they came). یکی here functions as 'one' meaning 'a certain person' or 'someone' who appears; followed by a noun phrase تیغ هندی به دست 'an Indian blade in (his) hand' — به + دست expresses instrument/location 'in/at the hand' (i.e. 'with in hand'). The two clauses are coordinate: first Faramarz came, and then a (different) man came with an Indian sword.
segments.s427-g61: برآویخت (a past-form verb) means 'sprang up' or 'pounced' and here takes با او 'upon/against him' as its complement. همی is an archaic/progressive particle used in Ferdowsi to give immediacy or continuative aspect ('straightaway/just then'). مهرنوش is the personal name and is the subject of the verb.
segments.s427-g62: دو رویه ز لشکر برآمد خروش is a compact descriptive clause: دو رویه ('two-faced' or 'two-sided' / 'on both sides') modifies the source of the cry; ز لشکر = 'from the army/host'; برآمد is the past 'arose/issued forth'; خروش = 'roar/cry/clamor.' Together: 'a two-sided cry arose from the host' (i.e. a shout from both ranks/sides).
segments.s427-g63: This noun phrase Gرامی دو پرخاشجوی جوان lists attributes before the head: گرامی 'honored/esteemed' qualifies the following phrase, دو marks the number 'two,' پرخاشجوی is an adjectival participle 'quarrelsome/aggressive,' and جوان here is a substantive 'young men.' The word order is typical Persian adjective-noun with numerals before the described noun.
segments.s427-g64: یکی ... دگر is a paired construction meaning 'one ... the other.' یکی 'one' introduces the first specification (شاهزاده = 'prince'), دگر 'the other' introduces the second (پهلوان = 'champion/hero'). No copula is expressed; these are noun appositions identifying the two youths.
segments.s427-g1: بدانگه که is a temporal connector ('thereupon/when') introducing the clause. رزم یلان is a genitive-like juxtaposition ('the battle of the heroes'), with the possessor یلان placed after رزم in classical word order. شد دراز is a verb + predicate adjective: شد (past of شدن, 'became') + دراز ('long'), so ‘became long/protracted.’
segments.s427-g2: همی is an iterative/continuous adverb here ('still/kept'); دیر شد is literal 'became late' (شد = became); R استم سرفراز is the name with an epithet in apposition (subject follows verb, common in epic Persian). The clause means Rostam remained/it grew late for Rostam, emphasizing duration.
segments.s427-g3: زواره is a place-name used adverbially ('to/at Zovareh') and precedes the verb in poetic order. بیاورد is 3rd-person past of آوردن ('he brought'). زان سو سپاه is a postposed qualifier for سپاه ('an army from that side'), where زان سو = 'from that side.'
segments.s427-g4: یکی is an indefinite/quantifying modifier ('one/a single') applied to لشکری ('a troop/army'); داغ‌دل and کینه‌خواه are compound adjectival epithets following the noun, giving attributes 'grief-stricken' and 'vengeful' respectively. The phrase describes the character of that host.
segments.s427-g5: به ایرانیان is an indirect-object prepositional phrase ('to the Iranians'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here used before the subject, as common in poetic inversion). رستم is the subject placed after the verb; کجاست is the interrogative clause 'where is (it)?' functioning as Rostam's question ('Where are you?').
segments.s427-g6: برین روز is a temporal phrase 'on this day' (بر + the demonstrative -ین forming classical برین = 'this'), with روز 'day' as the head. بیهوده modifies the action ('in vain'); خامش 'silent' describes the state; چراست is a fused question 'why is it?' tying the whole phrase into Rostam's reproach ('why are you silently wasting this day?').
segments.s427-g7: شما is the 2nd-person plural pronoun 'you'. سوی رستم is a directional phrase 'toward Rostam'. به جنگ آمدید is an idiom 'came to battle/you came to fight' with آمدید the 2nd-person-plural past of آمدن.
segments.s427-g8: خرامان is an adverb meaning 'with a stately/proud gait' modifying آمدید. به here is instrumental ('with'), and چنگ نهنگ is a genitival phrase 'the whale's claw' (چنگ 'claw' modified by نهنگ 'whale'). Together the clause says 'you came proudly, with the whale's claw.'
segments.s427-g121: بدو گفت رستم is classical word order: the prepositional object (بدو, “to him”) comes first, then the verb گفت (“said,” past 3rd person) and the subject رستم follows; literally “to him said Rostam.” کزین + گفت و گوی is a nominal phrase: کزین = “from/of this,” followed by گفت و گوی (“talk/discussion,” a noun phrase formed by two related nouns). Together they introduce the content of what Rostam says: “From this talk...”
segments.s427-g122: This clause asks about the consequence of that discussion. چه is the interrogative “what” (introducing the question). باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن used here to mean roughly “will be / come of” (classical modality, not the imperative in the dictionary list). مگر here has an adversative/conditional sense (“unless / except that” or rhetorically “is it not that”), and کم شود is a verb phrase meaning “become less / be diminished” (کم “little/less” + شود ‘become’). آبروی is the object “reputation / honour.”
segments.s427-g123: Two coordinated imperatives/hortatory phrases: به یزدان پناه = “take refuge in God” (به + noun + noun), و = “and,” then به یزدان گرای = “turn to God.” The preposition به precedes its noun as usual. گرای here is an imperative or jussive verb meaning “turn (to)” (an older/poetic verb form), so the whole segment is advice/command.
segments.s427-g124: که introduces an explanatory or causal clause “for/that,” and اویست is an archaic fused form corresponding to او + است (“he is”). بر here means “over / with regard to” and governs the domain نیک و بد (“good and bad”). رهنمای is a predicate noun “guide” (He is the guide with respect to good and bad). The clause thus gives the reason: ‘for He is the guide in matters of good and evil.’
segments.s427-g33: Verb‑initial clause typical in epic poetry: بکشتند is a past‑tense plural verb 'they killed'. ز here is the archaic/poetic form of از marking the source/partitive ('of/from among'), followed by the definite plural ایرانیان 'Iranians' and the postpositive adjective بی‌شمار 'countless'. The whole phrase functions as a complete clause 'they killed countless Iranians.'
segments.s427-g34: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. نوش‌آذر is the proper name (subject). آن is a deictic 'that' referring back to the preceding event. دید is the past tense 'he saw'. بر + ساخت here form a compound idea 'set about / took up' (بر indicating 'upon' or the start of an action and ساخت the past 'made/did'), and کار is the object 'deed/affair' — so 'when Nush‑Āzar saw that, he set about the deed.'
segments.s427-g35: سمند سرافراز is a noun + epithet (noun then adjective). را is the definite‑object/topic marker attaching to the noun phrase. بر نشست is a past action 'sat upon / mounted' where بر indicates 'upon' and نشست is the past of 'to sit/settle', so the clause reads 'he mounted the lofty steed.'
segments.s427-g36: بیامد is the past 'came' (verb first as is common in epic lines). یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / a man'. تیغ هندی is the noun with an adjective 'Indian sword'. به دست is an adverbial phrase 'with (in) hand' where به here means 'in/with' and دست is 'hand' — together: 'a man came, an Indian sword in (his) hand.'
segments.s427-g37: یکی نامور بود is a predicative structure: 'there was a certain famed one' (یکی = a certain/one, نامور = renowned, بود = was). الوای is an exclamatory particle 'lo!/behold!' and نام following it introduces 'name' — i.e. 'Lo! the name...'.
segments.s427-g38: A simple coordinate list of names/epithets linked by و 'and': سرافراز (epithet/name), اسپ‌افگن (a descriptive name, literally 'horse‑breaker' / 'unhorsing warrior'), و شادکام (and 'fortunate' or 'Shadkām' as epithet). The sequence gives the character's titles/names.
segments.s427-g39: کجا is the interrogative adverb 'where'. نیزه رستم is an example of Persian genitive juxtaposition 'Rostam's spear' (noun + possessor). او is a resumptive/pronominal reference to 'him' (Rostam) for emphasis, and داشتی is the second‑person past 'you had' — giving the rhetorical question 'Where did you have Rostam's spear?'.
segments.s427-g40: پس is a discourse connective 'then/so'. پشت او means 'his back / his rear' (پشت + pronoun او). هیچ نگذاشتی is a negative or emphatic expression 'you left nothing' (هیچ = nothing/none, نگذاشتی = you did not leave/you left), so the clause accuses: 'you left his rear with nothing (no protection).'
segments.s427-g105: فرامرز را is the object phrase (را is the definite/object marker) marking 'Faramarz' as the direct object; نیز ('also') adds that he too is included. بسته is a past-participle/adjectival form meaning 'bound/tied' and modifies the following noun phrase دو دست ('two hands' = 'both hands'), so the phrase literally reads 'Faramarz (object) also, with both hands bound.' The order (adjective before quantity+noun) is a poetic inversion but idiomatic.
segments.s427-g106: بیارم is the verb 'bring' in a first‑person form (classical/poetic form corresponding to 'I bring' / 'I will bring' or a hortative 'let me bring'); it governs the prepositional phrase بر شاه یزدان‌پرست 'to/before the God‑worshipping king'. بر here functions as 'to/toward' and یزدان‌پرست is a compound adjective 'God‑worshipping' qualifying شاه ('king').
segments.s427-g107: به خون + noun phrase is a prepositional/ablative construction here meaning 'with/ by (means of) blood' or idiomatically 'shed the blood of …'; گرانمایگانشان is a possessive compound 'their esteemed/noble ones' (گرانمایگان = 'nobles, esteemed ones' + شان 'their'), and بکش is the imperative 'kill!' directed at an understood agent. The clause is an abrupt imperative: 'Spill the blood of their nobles!'.
segments.s427-g108: مشوران here is a vocative/plural noun 'counselors' (addressing the advisers). ازین is the fused form of از + این ('from/of this/this') and رای means 'counsel/plan'; بیهوده qualifies that counsel as 'vain/pointless'. هش is an interjection meaning 'beware!' or an admonition. Together: 'Counselors, beware of this vain counsel/plan.'
segments.s427-g109: چنین 'thus' sets the manner, and گفت is the verb 'said' (here the past narrative 'he said'); با + name (با رستم) marks the addressee 'to/with Rostam' and اسفندیار is the speaker's name. Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis: 'Thus Esfandiyar said to Rostam.'
segments.s427-g110: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'that...'); بر کین here carries the sense 'for the sake of vengeance against' (بر + کین = 'against/for vengeance'), طاوس نر is a noun phrase 'the male peacock' (طاوس = peacock, نر = male), and خون مار 'the serpent's blood' uses noun + noun apposition where خون qualifies the object relating to مار ('the blood of the snake/serpent'). The clause is compressed/poetic: 'that, for vengeance on the male peacock, (shall be) the serpent's blood' (i.e., spill the serpent's blood to avenge the peacock).
segments.s427-g111: بریزیم is a first‑person plural/ hortative-subjunctive form of ریختن ('to pour/ to shed'), here used generically ('to shed' / 'if we were to shed'); ناخوب و ناخوش are descriptive adjectives 'bad/unseemly and unpleasant', and بود is the past copula 'would be / was' — together forming a conditional/opinion clause: 'To shed (blood) would be ugly and disagreeable.'
segments.s427-g112: نه at the clause start negates the preceding idea ('not'); آیین means 'custom/practice', شاهان is the plural 'kings', سرکش is the adjective 'rebellious', and بود is the past copula 'was'. Together: 'Nor was it the custom/practice of rebellious kings.' (i.e. such shedding is not the way of kings).
segments.s427-g81: اندر + noun/adverbial form gives a locative 'in/within' (an archaising preposition). تو is the 2nd-person subject ('you'). نبردی here is an adjectival/adverbial use built on نبرد ('battle') meaning 'in battle/engaged in fighting' (not a verbal past); و links the clauses, ما is the 1st-person plural subject, and پر ز + noun is the fixed phrase 'full of X' (پر = 'full', ز = 'of/from' in poetic register).
segments.s427-g82: A simple nominal phrase listing the sufferers: جوانان و کی‌زادگان 'the youths and the Kayanian-born/royal scions'. زیر + گرد is a poetic spatial phrase meaning 'under the dust' (گرد = dust) implying they lie fallen; word order is noun1 و noun2 + locative.
segments.s427-g83: بر + این = 'upon this' (a fused classical form 'برین') marks topical/locative 'on this'. تخمه here means 'seed/lineage/offspring' (i.e. this stock), این ننگ 'this shame'. تا introduces the scope/goal 'تا جاودان' = 'so that / until forever' (purpose/extent), with جاودان 'eternal'.
segments.s427-g84: بماند is a present/subjunctive-like or optative form 'may it remain' / 'let it remain' (classical use of بماند to express lasting). ز here is the poetic 'because of/from', کردار 'deeds/actions', نابخردان 'the foolish' (plural) — the agent causing the shame.
segments.s427-g85: دل + مرد is a nominal phrase 'the man's heart' (دل = heart/soul, مرد = man/person). بیدارتر شد is the comparative 'became more awake/alert' (بیدارتر = more awake), شد = 'became'. ز خشم uses the poetic preposition ز 'from/because of' to indicate cause: 'from anger.'
segments.s427-g86: Two parallel predicative phrases introduced by پر از 'full of': پر از تاب مغز = 'full of تاب (radiance/brightness) of the brain/mind' (a poetic way to say 'mind full of brilliance'); و joins the parallel, پر از آب چشم = 'full of the water of the eye' — an idiom for 'eyes full of tears.' از here is the partitive/relational 'of.'
segments.s427-g87: به رستم 'to/for Rostam' marks the indirect object/recipient. چنین گفت is the formula 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so'); کای is a vocative 'O' (an archaic/epic form) addressing Rostam. بدنشان 'their bodies/their persons' is a possessive compound (بدن + شان).
segments.s427-g88: The reporter/quotation continues: چنین بود 'thus/so was' (بود here = past 'was'), پیمان 'the pact/pledge/covenant', گردنکشان an epithet meaning 'the rebels' or 'neck-stretchers' (proud or rebellious ones). Word order is topical/emphatic: 'such was the covenant of the rebels.'
segments.s427-g41: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause; از دور is an adverbial phrase 'from afar' placed before the object. نوش‌آذر او را is the definite object ('Nowsh‑Azar' + the object marker را) and بدید is a past tense verb 'saw' at the end of the clause (typical Persian SOV order with the verb final).
segments.s427-g42: بزد is a past tense verb 'he struck/struck (at)'; دست (without a possessive) is the object 'hand' → 'he struck with his hand'. The conjunction و links the next action: تیغ (sword) is the object of برکشید, and the adverbial phrase از میان 'out of/from between' modifies برکشید 'drew/raised' (here 'drew his sword out'). برکشید is past tense, used here to mean 'drew (a weapon)'.
segments.s427-g43: یکی here means 'one/once' (one stroke) and modifies the action تیغ زد 'struck with the sword'. The verb زد is past tense 'struck', and بر introduces the target 'on/upon' (بر سر و گردنش = 'on his head and neck'). گردن takes the enclitic ش for 'his'.
segments.s427-g44: بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase 'to him' (indicating the result befell him). نیمه شد is a resultative construction 'became half' or 'was made half' (شد = 'became/was'). پیل‌پیکر تنش is a noun phrase 'his elephant‑bodied body' with پیل‌پیکر as a prefixed adjectival compound and ش the possessive enclitic 'his'.
segments.s427-g45: زواره is the proper name (subject). برانگیخت is past tense 'he stirred/roused/raised'. اسپ نبرد is a compound noun 'war‑horse' (اسپ = horse, نبرد qualifying it as battle/war‑horse) and functions as the object of برانگیخت; Persian often omits the possessive pronoun when ownership is obvious from context.
segments.s427-g46: به تندی is an adverbial phrase formed by به + noun meaning 'with haste/quickly' (به here marks the adverbial, i.e., 'by/with -ly'). The second به introduces the indirect object 'to Nowsh‑Azar' (به نوش‌آذر), آواز کرد is a light‑verb construction 'uttered a call / called' where آواز is the noun 'call/voice' and کرد is the auxiliary 'made/did' (past).
segments.s427-g47: که introduces a content/clause (here marking reported speech or an imperative sense). او را is the definite object marker 'him'. فگندی is an archaic/poetic form (variant of فکندی / فکن) used here in an optative/imperatival sense 'cast/throw' or 'put down'; کنون 'now' is an adverb placed after the verb for emphasis, and پای دار is a noun phrase 'the foot/base of the dar' (دار here meaning 'gallows' or literally 'tree' in poetic contexts).
segments.s427-g48: چو here works as a conjunction 'as/for' introducing a comment about the following phrase. الوای (الوا) is an interjection/term invoked here (the exact literary sense is archaising/poetic); را after it marks it as the topical/emphasized item. من نخوانم is negative present/subjunctive 'I do not (or would not) call'—ن‑ + خوان + م—where the verb 'خواندن' is used in the sense 'to call/summon'. سوار is the predicate noun 'rider/horseman'. The clause thus states roughly 'as for X, I would not call him a rider.'
segments.s427-g9: همی is an emphatic/continuous adverb prefacing the verb phrase. دست رستم is the direct object phrase ('Rostam's hand') with the noun دست before the name (typical word order in poetry). نخواهید is the (negative) second-person plural present/future form of خواستن/خواست (here functioning as 'will not' / 'cannot'), and بست (from بستن) means 'to bind' — together نخواهید بست says 'you will not bind' or 'you cannot bind'.
segments.s427-g10: برین is the fused form of بر + این = 'on this'; رزمگه is the noun 'battlefield'. نشاید is an impersonal/modal present meaning 'it is not fitting/should not', and نشست is the infinitive/masdar sense 'to sit' (here 'to sit/settle' = 'sit idle'). The clause thus reads literally 'on this battlefield it is not fitting to sit.'
segments.s427-g11: زواره is a proper name here (subject). به دشنام is an adverbial prepositional phrase 'with/into insult' (به + دشنام). لب برگشاد is a verb phrase 'opened his lip(s)' (لب = 'lip', برگشاد = 'opened') — the object-like phrase 'لب' appears before the verb as is common in poetic word order.
segments.s427-g12: همی کرد gives a continuous or iterative aspect ('kept doing' / 'continually did'). کرد is the light verb 'to do/make' taking the following nominal phrase as its complement. گفتار ناخوب یاد is the object: گفتار ناخوب = 'bad speech/words', and یاد here functions as a noun meaning 'mention/recall' so that the whole phrase means 'he uttered/spoke bad words.'
segments.s427-g13: برآشفت is a past/punctual verb meaning 'he became angry' or 'he flew into rage'. ازان = از + آن 'from that / because of that' (adverbial cause), پور is the patronymic/epithet 'son (of)' used before a proper name, and اسفندیار is the proper name; together the phrase is 'from that, the son Esfandiyār became angry.'
segments.s427-g14: This is a nominal apposition describing Esfandiyār: سواری 'a mounted rider/warrior' is the head, modified by the adjective cluster بد اسپ‌افگن 'bad/terrible (in the sense of) horse‑unseater' (بد here is attributive/intensifying and اسپ‌افگن is a compound noun 'horse‑unseater'), and و نامدار adds a further epithet 'and renowned.' The adjectives are coordinated by و and follow the head noun in typical poetic listing.
segments.s427-g15: جوانی 'a youth / a young man' is followed by the relative clause که ... بود giving identifying information. نوش آذرش is a name phrase: نوش آذر is treated as the personal name and the -ش is the third‑person possessive 'his' (i.e. 'his Nush‑Āzar'), and بود is the copula 'was' — the construction نوش آذرش بود نام literally 'the name was Nush‑Āzar' = 'whose name was Nush‑Āzar.'
segments.s427-g16: A sequence of predicate adjectives coordinated by و: سرافراز 'honored/proud', جنگاور 'warrior/war‑like', and شادکام 'fortunate/prosperous'. In Persian poetry such adjective lists follow the noun they describe and serve as its appositive predicates.
segments.s427-g49: Subject (proper name) + numeral phrase + object + verb: زواره (proper name) is the subject; یکی modifies نیزه and means “one/a single” (i.e. one spear). نیزه is the direct object and زد is a simple past active verb “struck/ thrust.” بر is the preposition “against/at,” and برش here functions as the target (object) — historically this is the preverbal preposition + pronominal form (the -ش is the 3rd‑person suffix) marking ‘him,’ though the provided lemma list does not include that pronoun sense.
segments.s427-g50: Adverbial phrase of direction + verb + temporal adverb + possessed noun: به + خاک اندر آمد is a classical/poetic way to say “came/fell to the earth” (به ... آمد = moved into/towards the ground). اندر is an archaic/prepositional form meaning “in/into.” همانگه is an adverb “at that moment/instantly.” سرش is سر (head) with the pronominal suffix -ش = “his head.”
segments.s427-g51: Temporal subordinate introduced by چو meaning “when.” نوش‌آذر is a proper name; نامور is an epithet/adjective placed after the name meaning “renowned.” کشته شد is a passive/perfect phrase: کُشته (slain) + شد used as the auxiliary ‘was’ (passive), so “was slain.”
segments.s427-g52: Main clause: سپه را places the army/topic marked by را (definite/object/topic marker). همه روز is a temporal phrase “the whole day/all day.” برگشته شد is a perfective/auxiliary construction meaning “returned/turned back” (برگشته + شد, with شد functioning as auxiliary/passive marker). Word order (topic + time + verb) is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s427-g53: برادرش is برادر + pronominal suffix -ش = “his brother.” گریان is a present participial/adjectival form meaning “weeping” used as a predicative adjective. و links to the next description; دل پر ز جوش literally “heart full of (پر) from/of (ز) joosh,” where جوش in this context means emotional surge/ardor. The construction describes emotional state: “his brother was weeping, and his heart was full of passion.”
segments.s427-g54: جوانی is a noun “a youth/young man.” که is the relative pronoun “who/that.” بد نام is a compound adjective ‘bad‑named’ = ‘ill‑reputed’; او is the 3rd‑person pronoun “his/her” (here linking the reputation to the person being described), and مهرنوش is a proper name placed as the thing/person identified with that reputation. The phrase reads literally as “a youth who — his bad name was Mehrnūsh.”
segments.s427-g55: غمی شد is an impersonal/experiential phrase: غم + شد = “a sorrow arose/occurred.” دل مرد شمشیرزن strings the possessed noun first: دل (heart) + مرد (man/hero) + شمشیرزن (swordsman) — i.e. “the heart of the man, the swordsman” — so the line means “a sorrow came upon the heart of the swordsman.”
segments.s427-g56: برانگیخت is the simple past verb “aroused/roused.” آن باره is an adverbial phrase “on that occasion/at that instant.” پیلتن is a proper name (person or place) serving as the object/experiencer of the arousal — “that occasion roused Pīltan.” Poetic word order places the verb first for emphasis.
segments.s427-g25: اگر is the conditional particle 'if' that opens a protasis. جنگ here is the noun 'war/battle' functioning with the verb کنید (i.e. 'to wage/engage in war'); بر نادرستی is an adverbial/prepositional phrase 'by/through wrongness' (i.e. 'unjustly'). کنید is the present/jussive form of کردن used for second-person plural (or polite singular) and gives the action 'you do/wage'.
segments.s427-g26: به کار اندرون is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in/to the inner affair(s)' (به + کار + اندرون); پیش‌دستی is a noun 'pre-emption/initiative' that serves as the thing to be performed, and کنید is again the verb 'do/perform' in the jussive/imperative sense 'take (the) initiative'. The noun phrase order (به کار اندرون پیش‌دستی) follows poetic/classical patterns where the action-noun precedes or directly combines with the verb.
segments.s427-g27: ببینید is an imperative/jussive verb 'see/observe' addressed to second-person plural. پیکار is the noun 'battle/combat' and جنگاوران ('warriors') follows it in a genitive/possessive relation (classical poetry often omits the ezāfe), so the phrase reads 'see the battle of the warriors.'
segments.s427-g28: This is an instrumental list introduced by the preposition به meaning 'by/with' (here marking instruments). تیغ 'sword' and سنان 'spear' are coordinated by و 'and'; then another به introduces گرز گران, where گرز 'mace/club' is modified postpositively by گران 'heavy' (a typical adjective placement in older Persian). The whole phrase specifies the weapons used in the combat.
segments.s427-g29: زواره is the proper name (subject). بفرمود is an honorific/past-tense verb 'he commanded/said' (classical polite past). کاندر ('ک + اندر' fused) means 'in which/therein' and introduces the subordinate clause; نهید here is the present/jussive form of نهاد/نَهَدن 'to place/set' used in reported command ('that they put / place').
segments.s427-g30: سران را is the definite object marked by را ('the leaders' or 'the heads'); ز خون is an ablative/qualifying phrase 'with blood' (i.e. 'bloodied'); بر سر افسر is a locative expression 'upon the top of the helmet' (بر = 'on', سر = 'top/head', افسر = 'helmet' here); the clause ends with نهید, repeating the verb 'place' so the full command reads 'place the leaders (heads), bloodied, on the helmets.'
segments.s427-g31: زواره is the subject (proper name). بیامد is the past tense 'came/arrived'. به پیش سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/in the presence of the army' (به = 'to/towards', پیش = 'before/in front of/in the presence of', سپاه = 'army'), indicating where he came.
segments.s427-g32: دهاده is a collective adjective/noun 'numerous' or 'many (a tenfold)'; it functions as the subject here. برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز آوردگاه ('from the battlefield') gives the source (ز = 'from', آور دگاه = 'battlefield'), so this clause means 'a multitude came forth from the battlefield.'
segments.s427-g113: تو‌ای is a vocative 'O you' (poetic address). بدنشان is بدن + ـشان, literally 'their body/person' used appositively to describe the addressee; چاره خویش ساز is an imperative clause: چاره (object 'remedy/solution') + خویش (possessive 'one's own', here split in the manuscript across خوی + ش) + ساز (imperative 'make' — poetic/elliptical form). Word order places the object (چاره) before the verb (ساز), which is normal in Persian for emphasis.
segments.s427-g114: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here ('for/because'); آمد is perfect/simple past used impersonally with زمانت ('your time has come'); به + تنگی expresses entering a state ('into narrowness/distress'), and فراز is an emphatic noun modifying تنگی ('to a high/acute degree'). The clause gives the reason for the preceding imperative.
segments.s427-g115: بر + رخش = 'upon his steed' (بر = on/upon, رخش = steed). با هردو introduces accompaniment/instrument ('with both'), here referring to two items (reins or similar riding-gear). به تیر is idiomatic for 'to/for the arrow/armed action' (به marks direction/instrument); the whole segment sets up the action of mounting and arming.
segments.s427-g116: برآمیزم is a first-person singular verb in the present-future sense 'I will rise/launch myself'; اکنون = 'now'; چو = 'as/like' (comparative particle); با + آب + شیر = a prepositional phrase 'with water/life and a lion' metrical/poetic image: شیر here is 'lion' and آب is 'water' (so 'like a lion at the water' — i.e. springing eagerly). The clause compares the speaker's imminent movement to that animal image.
segments.s427-g117: بدان is the imperative 'know this' (here split as ب + دان in the word list; ب is the verbal prefix and دان = 'know'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that'; کس 'anyone' (used generically); از بندگان زین = 'of/from the servants (of) the saddle' — زین = 'saddle'—meaning the mounted retainers; سپس = 'afterward'. The segment sets the purpose/result for the following verb.
segments.s427-g118: نجویند is a third-person plural form meaning 'they will not/shall not seek/speak to' in this formulaic negative-potential sense (context gives the negative idea 'no one will seek'); کین = 'vengeance/hostility' (the object sought); خداوند here is 'the lord/sovereign' (not God); کس = 'anyone'. Together: 'no one will seek vengeance against the lord.'
segments.s427-g119: وگر = 'if' (conditional); زنده = 'alive'; مانی here stands for the verb 'you remain' (classical omission of the b- prefix: بمانی → مانی), i.e. second-person present/subjunctive — this form is not treated as the name 'Mani' in the provided senses; ببندمت is 'I will bind/tie you' (بـ-...-ت enclitic expresses 'you' as object), and چنگ here is physical 'claw/grip' (not the musical instrument). The clause is a conditional threat.
segments.s427-g120: به نزدیک = 'to/near' (به + نزدیک, marking motion toward proximity); شاهت = شاه + ـت 'your king' (possessive suffix); برم is first-person singular 'I will carry/take' (برد sense 'take/carry'); بی‌درنگ = adverb 'without delay/at once'. The clause states the resulting action: the speaker will bring the bound person before the king immediately.
segments.s427-g97: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'he heard' with verb-before-subject order (typical in epic style); رستم is the subject. غمی گشت is a predicative phrase 'became sorrow/grief' (گشت = 'became'); سخت intensifies that state (here 'severely' / 'deeply').
segments.s427-g98: بلرزید is past 'he/it trembled'. برسان here is used in the classical/poetic sense 'به‌سان' = 'like' (a simile marker) followed by the noun phrase شاخ درخت 'the bough (branch) of the tree'.
segments.s427-g99: به introduces the oath's objects ('by'). جان و سر شاه is a fixed formula 'by the life and head (honor) of the king'. سوگند خورد is an idiom 'to swear an oath' where خورد functions as a light verb in past tense (classical usage: 'took/swore an oath').
segments.s427-g100: This is a continued list introduced by به: خورشید و شمشیر و دشت نبرد — 'by the sun, the sword, and the plain of battle' (dشت نبرد = battlefield). The nouns are coordinated with و ('and').
segments.s427-g101: که introduces reported content ('that'). من جنگ هرگز sets the theme 'I (of) war never...'. نفرموده‌ام is negative perfect of فرمودن (ن + فرموده‌ام) and means 'I have not commanded/ordered' — an archaic/classical negative perfect construction.
segments.s427-g102: This is a relative/continuation: کسی کین چنین کرد = 'anyone who did such a deed of vengeance'. کسی is indefinite 'anyone', کین 'vengeance' modifies the action, چنین 'thus/such', کرد is simple past 'did'. نستوده‌ام is negative perfect of ستودن (ن + ستوده‌ام) = 'I have not praised'.
segments.s427-g103: ببندم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive/optative form of بستن used here with future/intentive force 'I will bind' or 'let me bind'. دو دست برادر is the direct object 'the brother's two hands' (دو دست = two hands; برادر = brother/kinsman). کنون is an adverb 'now'. Word order verb → object → adverb is typical in poetry.
segments.s427-g104: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. او بود is past 'he was'. اندر بدی is a prepositional phrase 'into/among evil' (i.e., into wrongdoing). رهنمون is 'guide' — together: 'if he was a guide into evil' (i.e. if he led him into wrongdoing).
segments.s427-g17: برآشفت is a past, poetic/perfective form meaning 'became angry' (verb first, a common poetic inversion). با + سگزی gives the cause or object of his anger ('at/with such doggishness' — سگزی here = base/doglike behavior). آن نامدار is the subject ('that renowned one'), placed after the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s427-g18: زبان را is the idiom 'open the tongue' (زبان = speech/utterance; را marks it as definite object). به دشنام بگشاد = 'opened (it) into/for an insult' (به indicating movement/turning into the act of insulting; بگشاد = opened/let loose). خوار here characterizes the insult — 'despicable' — i.e. the word he uttered.
segments.s427-g19: چنین گفت is a straightforward 'he said thus/so' (چنین modifies گفت). کآری گو should be read as a compressed clause = که آری گو ('that say “yes”'), where گو is the imperative/infinitive 'say' and کآری (here functioning with که) indicates the word 'آری' ('yes'); برمنش is the indirect object or addressee 'to the noble‑minded one'. Word order is elliptical/condensed, typical of epic diction.
segments.s427-g20: به فرمان شاهان is a prepositional phrase 'by the command of kings' (به here = 'by'). کند is the verb 'make/do' (here causative: 'cause him to be/act'); بدکنش is a nominal/epithet meaning 'ill‑behaved' or 'one who does ill' — together 'cause him to become an ill‑doer/act wickedly'.
segments.s427-g21: نفرمود here functions as a speech/command verb in an inverted order: verb + indirect object + subject (poetic inversion). ما را is the object/recipient 'to us', and یل اسفندیار is the subject 'the hero Esfandiyar' (he who gives or, as here, does not give the order).
segments.s427-g22: چنین modifies the preceding verb (i.e. 'in this way' or 'thus'). با سگان ساختن کارزار is an infinitival phrase 'to make battle with dogs' (با = with/against; سگان here = 'dogs' in the derogatory sense, i.e. scoundrels; ساختن کارزار = 'to make/create a battle' — literally 'to wage war').
segments.s427-g23: که begins a subordinate/relative clause 'that/so that'. پیچد is present/subjunctive/poetic form of 'to turn' or 'to be turned' (meaning 'turns away/changes'). سر از رای و فرمان او literally 'the head from his counsel and command' — idiomatically 'his mind/loyalty departs from his counsel and orders'.
segments.s427-g24: که introduces the second parallel clause. یارد 'companion' (friend or ally) is the subject, گذشتن ز (گذشتن + ز) = 'to pass away from/violate' (i.e. to break a bound), and پیمان او 'his covenant' — so 'that a companion breaks his covenant with him.'
segments.s427-g73: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' (not a simile here). بهمن is the subject (proper name). برادرش combines برادر 'brother' with the enclitic possessive ش 'his' (so 'his brother'); را is the definite/direct-object marker attached after the object. کشته is a past participle 'killed/slain' functioning as object/complement, and دید is the past tense verb 'saw' placed at the end (typical Persian word order: [time/aux] + subject + object + participle + verb).
segments.s427-g74: زمین 'the ground/earth' is the noun; زیر او is a prepositional phrase 'beneath him' (زیر = 'under/beneath', او = 'he/him'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as', followed by گل آغشته 'a flower/flower-like thing soaked/tinged' (گل as the comparison noun, آغشته a participial adjective 'soaked/tinged'). دید is the main verb 'saw' again; the whole clause is another object of 'saw' (i.e. 'he saw that the ground beneath him was like a flower soaked').
segments.s427-g75: بیامد is the simple past 'he came'. دوان (literally 'running' or 'in haste') is an adverbial/participial element modifying بیامد, so the phrase means 'he came running' or 'he ran up'. نزد (here) means 'to/near, in the presence of' and introduces the person reached: اسفندیار (proper name).
segments.s427-g76: به جایی 'to a place' uses به as the preposition 'to/toward' and جایی 'place/spot'. که بود introduces a relative clause 'that/where ... was' (که = 'that/where'; بود = past of 'to be'). آتش کارزار is a genitive phrase 'the fire of the battle' or 'the fires of battle' (آتش = 'fire', کارزار = 'battle/field'). Altogether: 'to the place where the fire of battle was'.
segments.s427-g77: بدو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to him' (بد + او contracted) here ('he said to him'). گفت is used as the reporting verb 'said' (the provided sense list lacked the verb sense, so the exact listed senses do not match the use here). کای is a vocative particle 'O'. The following words form a string of epithets: نره 'male/virile', شیر 'lion', ژیان an epithet often translated 'life/spirited' or 'vital' — together they function as an address: 'O manly, lion-hearted one.'
segments.s427-g78: سپاهی is 'an army/army contingent' or 'soldiers' (subject of the embedded statement). به جنگ آمد is a verbal phrase 'came to battle' (به + جنگ + آمد). از marks origin 'from', followed by سگزیان which here refers to a people/group (the Sagzians) rather than the lexical senses given — because the listed senses are about a resin and do not fit the context, that token's sense is marked as not applicable.
segments.s427-g79: دو پور is 'two sons' (دو = two, پور = son/element in names but here 'sons'); تو here follows the noun as a possessive 'your' (classical/poetic word order often places the possessor after the noun). نوش‌آذر و مهرنوش are proper names of the two sons joined by و 'and'.
segments.s427-g80: به خواری is an adverbial phrase 'into humiliation/in disgrace' (به = 'into/to', خواری = 'humiliation/disgrace'). به سگزی is another prepositional phrase 'to the Sagzians' or 'into the hands of the Sagzi' (here سگزی refers to a group/place; the listed lexical senses for سگزی do not match that use, so it is marked as inapplicable). سپردند is the past plural verb 'they handed over/entrusted'. هوش ('mind/awareness') appears as the final noun — in context it marks what was delivered (their welfare/subjective sense) or can be read idiomatically as 'they gave them over, depriving them of sense/awareness' — grammatically, هوش is the thing affected/mentioned after سپردند.
segments.s427-g89: تو گفتی is a past tense clause: تو (you) + گفتی (you said). که introduces reported speech ('that'). لشکر is the direct object placed before the verb, and نیارم is an archaic/subjunctive negative form meaning 'that I not bring' (classical Persian نیاورم used after گفتی to report a command or counsel). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'to/into battle' giving direction.
segments.s427-g90: ترا نیست is a verbless-possession construction: ترا (to you / you have) + نیست ('is not') → 'you do not have'. آرایش (adornment/array) governs the following genitive phrase نام و ننگ ('name and shame' = fame and disgrace). Word order is topical: object/person (ترا) before the predicate (نیست) and the thing absent (آرایش ...) after it.
segments.s427-g91: نداری here is the negative present 'you do not have' (ن + داری). ز (poetic) means 'from/before' and links to the following noun: ز من شرم = 'shame before me'. وز is a fused poetic sequence (= و + ز) meaning 'and from/and before', so وز کردگار = 'and (shame/fear) before the Creator/God'. Note the classical word order: verb (نداری) can appear before its complements.
segments.s427-g92: نترسی is second-person present negative (ن + ترسی) meaning 'you do not fear' or 'you need not fear'. که introduces the subordinate clause explaining what is not feared: پرسند is a classical present (3rd plural) 'they ask' (here best read as 'they will ask' / 'they ask for an account'), and روز شمار is a compound 'day-count' meaning 'the day's reckoning' or 'the Day of Account'.
segments.s427-g93: ندانی is the negative present/question form 'you do not know' (ن + دانی). که introduces the content clause. مردان is the plural noun 'men', and پیمان‌شکن is a compound adjective/noun 'oath-breaker' modifying مردان ('men who break their oaths').
segments.s427-g94: ستوده نباشد is an impersonal negative predicate: ستوده 'praised' + نباشد (classical negative present/subjunctive form) → 'is not praised' or 'ought not to be praised'. بر انجمن means 'before/in the assembly' (بر = on/upon/at, انجمن = assembly/council). The phrase as a whole states that oath‑breakers are not to be praised in public assembly.
segments.s427-g95: دو is the numeral 'two'. سگزی is an attributive adjective here meaning 'base/doglike' (an insult) modifying the following noun. دو پور literally 'two sons' (dual numeral + پور 'son'). مرا کشته‌اند is the perfect 3rd plural 'they have killed' with مرا as the object pronoun ('my ...' in older usage the object form can mark the kin‑term's possession); together the clause reports that two contemptible men killed the speaker's two sons. (Note: some morphological items are contracted/poetic; classical word order allows numerals and adjectives before kin nouns.)
segments.s427-g96: بران is a prepositional/adverbial 'on that / because of that' referring back to the previous fact. خیرگی is a noun 'astonishment; stupefaction' (what they caused or what followed), and باز برگشته‌اند is the perfect 'they have returned' with باز 'again' as an adverb before the verb. The whole clause says the killers have returned again in that state or because of that amazement.

sentences.s427-s33: When the warlike lions rushed at one another, they kept striking at each other's heads.
sentences.s427-s34: At the place of combat Mehrnush became keen/alert; she was not there with Faramarz.
sentences.s427-s35: He struck with his sword at the neck of his horse; he cast the head before the swift‑footed one.
sentences.s427-s36: Faramarz made him fall, ruined and on foot; from the blood the battlefield's earth became ruby.
sentences.s427-s29: He moved out from the midst of the host to the front of the ranks; from the anguish of his heart he brought foam to his lips.
sentences.s427-s30: From that side Faramarz came forward like a maddened elephant; a man came with an Indian sword in his hand.
sentences.s427-s31: Mehrnush leapt upon him; from the host a cry rose up from both ranks.
sentences.s427-s32: Two honored, quarrelsome young men — one a prince, the other a champion.
sentences.s427-s1: When the heroes' battle grew long, Rostam the proud remained for a long time.
sentences.s427-s2: He brought a host to Zovareh — from that side an army: a single troop, grief-stricken and bent on vengeance.
sentences.s427-s3: Rostam said to the Iranians, 'Where are you? Why are you silently wasting this day in vain?'
sentences.s427-s4: You came toward Rostam to battle; you advanced proudly, bearing the whale's claw.
sentences.s427-s61: Rostam said to him, "What will come of this talk? Will it not lessen one's reputation?"
sentences.s427-s62: Take refuge in God and turn to God, for He is the guide in both good and bad.
sentences.s427-s17: They killed countless Iranians; when Nush‑Āzar saw that, he set about the deed.
sentences.s427-s18: He mounted the lofty steed; a man came with an Indian sword in his hand.
sentences.s427-s19: There was a famed one — lo! his name was Srafraz, Esp‑afgan, and Shadkām.
sentences.s427-s20: Where was Rostam's spear? You left his rear with nothing (no one) to protect it.
sentences.s427-s53: I will also bring Faramarz with both hands bound before the God‑worshipping king.
sentences.s427-s54: Shed the blood of their nobles! — Counselors, beware of this vain plan.
sentences.s427-s55: Thus Esfandiyar said to Rostam: 'For vengeance upon the male peacock, (we must) spill the serpent's blood.'
sentences.s427-s56: To shed it would be ugly and unpleasant; it was not the custom of rebellious kings.
sentences.s427-s41: You are in battle, while we are full of sorrow — the young men and royal scions lie beneath the dust.
sentences.s427-s42: May this shame remain upon this line forever because of the deeds of the foolish.
sentences.s427-s43: The man's heart grew more alert with anger; his mind was full of radiance and his eyes full of water (tears).
sentences.s427-s44: He said to Rostam thus: 'O Rostam — their bodies were like that; such was the covenant of the rebels.'
sentences.s427-s21: When (he) saw Nowsh‑Azar from afar, he struck with his hand and drew his sword out from between (his side).
sentences.s427-s22: With one stroke the sword struck at his head and neck; the elephant‑bodied man's body was cleft in two.
sentences.s427-s23: Zavāreh roused (his) war‑horse and, swiftly, called out to Nowsh‑Azar.
sentences.s427-s24: “That (you) now throw him at the foot of the gallows; as for 'Alwā', I would not call him a rider.”
sentences.s427-s5: You will not bind Rostam's hand; on this battlefield it is not fitting to sit (idle).
sentences.s427-s6: Zovāreh opened his lips in insult; he continually spoke ugly words.
sentences.s427-s7: From that, the son Esfandiyār grew angry; a mounted warrior, a terrible horse‑unseater and famed.
sentences.s427-s8: A young man whose name was Nush‑Āzar — lofty (honored), a warrior, and fortunate.
sentences.s427-s25: Zavāreh struck with a single spear at him; his head at that instant fell to the ground.
sentences.s427-s26: When renowned Nush-Āzar was slain, the host turned back and withdrew for the whole day.
sentences.s427-s27: His brother wept, his heart full of boiling ardor; a youth whose ill name was Mehrnūsh.
sentences.s427-s28: A sorrow came upon the heart of the sword‑man; that event stirred Pīltan.
sentences.s427-s13: If you make war unjustly, take the initiative in internal affairs.
sentences.s427-s14: Behold the warriors' combat with swords and spears and with heavy maces.
sentences.s427-s15: Zowareh commanded: 'Place within it the leaders' heads, bloodied, upon the helmets.'
sentences.s427-s16: Zowareh came before the army; a great multitude issued forth from the battlefield.
sentences.s427-s57: You — make a remedy for them yourself, for your time has come to severe distress.
sentences.s427-s58: I will mount my steed, taking both reins, and now I will spring forth like a lion to the water.
sentences.s427-s59: Know this, so that thereafter no one of the servants from the saddle will seek vengeance against this lord.
sentences.s427-s60: And if you remain alive, I will seize you by the claw and immediately bring you near your king.
sentences.s427-s49: When Rostam heard, he was stricken with grief and trembled like the bough of a tree.
sentences.s427-s50: He swore by the king's life and head, and by the sun, the sword, and the battlefield.
sentences.s427-s51: Saying that I have never ordered war; I have not praised anyone who has carried out such vengeance.
sentences.s427-s52: I will bind the brother's two hands now, if he was the one who led him into wrongdoing.
sentences.s427-s9: That famed man grew angry at such baseness and opened his mouth to hurl an insult, calling (him) 'despicable'.
sentences.s427-s10: He said thus: 'Say “yes” to the noble‑minded one; by the kings' command make him act ill.'
sentences.s427-s11: The hero Esfandiyar did not command us thus to make war with dogs.
sentences.s427-s12: — that his head (mind) turns away from his counsel and command, — that a companion departs from his covenant with him.
sentences.s427-s37: When Bahman saw his brother slain, he saw the ground beneath him soaked as if with flowers.
sentences.s427-s38: He came running to Esfandiyar, to the spot where the fires of battle were.
sentences.s427-s39: He said to him, 'O manly, lion-hearted one—an army has come to fight from the Sagzians.'
sentences.s427-s40: Your two sons, Nush-Āzar and Mehrnush, they delivered into humiliation and into the hands of the Sagzians.
sentences.s427-s45: You said that I should not bring an army to battle; you have no adornment of name and shame.
sentences.s427-s46: You have no shame before me and no fear of the Creator; you do not fear that they will ask for the day’s reckoning.
sentences.s427-s47: Do you not know that men who break their oaths are not praised in the assembly?
sentences.s427-s48: Two base men have killed my two sons; because of that astonishment they have returned.
